segments.label: Cold open: the snapback theory
segments.status: MINED (verified vs VTT; start at 'the first mouse, the innovator, buys it')
segments.why: Most vivid storytelling in the assigned sources; the 10-day-high reward anchor ties it straight to the 5DD frame.
segments.quote_anchor: the first mouse, the innovator, buys it because it's sold off so hard; he figures... what could possibly go wrong. The second mouse waits for a little bit of evidence that the turnaround has actually occurred... measuring the amount of risk he's taking versus the reward of getting back to the 10-day high

alternates.label: 5DD thesis in his voice ('reasons to believe this could be an abnormally good move')

segments.label: EXPLAINER: the rule in one picture
segments.vo_script: DRAFT: Five days. Each close lower than the last. That is the whole signal. The fifth bar is the signal day. The entry is a buy stop just above its high: the market must come UP to get you in. The void is just below its low: if price touches there first, the setup is dead and you never enter. The distance between them is your R, one unit of risk, defined before you ever own a share.
segments.animation_brief: Five candles close lower one by one (count chips 1..5 land per close); entry stop line draws above bar 5's high on 'buy stop just above its high'; void line below its low; the R bracket spans them on 'one unit of risk'. Schematic candles, same visual family as the frog-box band chart.

segments.label: OVERDUB: him walking a live 5DD
segments.status: MINED (verified vs VTT; optional lead-in from 1762s 'here's another 5dd... Devon Energy... it is so violent')
segments.animation_brief: Keep his audio. Animate Devon Energy's actual bars (pull real OHLC from quotes_eod for the session date once the video date is confirmed in cutroom): five bars count down as he counts aloud, yellow zone draws on 'my trade frame becomes this yellow zone', entry line +5c above the high and void -5c below the low land as he names them, 1R bracket, then the reward arrow back to the 10-day high on 'that becomes my reward'.
segments.why: Names the symbol, counts the five bars aloud, states entry/void, computes reward-to-risk — one continuous 85s take. The heavy on-screen pointing is exactly what an overdub replaces.
segments.quote_anchor: fifth day down, fourth day down, third day down, second day down, first day down... my trade frame becomes this yellow zone. I calculate the mechanical entry as five cents above today's high, mechanical sale five cents below today's low... that's a 1R mechanical risk... back to the 10-day high... probably three and a half to four to one, Devon Energy

alternates.label: Boeing compact walkthrough ('that's exactly what a 5DD looks like')
whiteboard mechanics (zero day, five lower closes, entry/void)

segments.label: WHAT HAPPENED: one instance, bar by bar (TSLA April 2023)
segments.vo_script: DRAFT (mirrors the verified data below): signal 2023-04-10 after five lower closes; filled next day; gapped through the void on earnings week; the fill was the open, not the stop. Minus two point two six R, not minus one. If this week looks familiar: it is the same Tesla earnings gap from the frog box video, seen from the other side.

verdict.how: gap through void, filled at open

segments.editorial_rules: frame-vs-price only (never Ken's personal P&L); synth voice only; publish outcome for EVERY featured frame; scheduled catalysts disclosed on screen; split-adjusted bars only; ambiguous daily bars counted and disclosed

segments.label: WHAT HAPPENED: every instance since 2020
segments.vo_script: DRAFT: We ran the rule, exactly as stated, on five liquid names from twenty-twenty forward. Eighty-five signals. Forty filled. Forty-one voided before entry: the rule said no. Average result on the fills, plus zero point seven six R. Half won. The best made seven point seven R. The worst lost two and a quarter, on a gap. Four signals were ambiguous on daily bars, so we counted them and left them out rather than guess. And the symbols disagree: Apple paid nearly one point eight R on average. The Q's lost money. Same rule. Different tape.

data.method: 5 consecutive lower closes; entry buy-stop +5bps above signal high; void -5bps below signal low; fill = max(entry, next open); stop = void; exit = stop or close of 10th session after fill; split-adjusted bars

segments.editorial_rules: frame-vs-price only; synth voice only; publish every outcome; split-adjusted bars only; ambiguous bars counted and disclosed

segments.label: FRAME THE FAILURE: January 2022, the first dip-buy of the bear
segments.vo_script: DRAFT: Now the honest part. January tenth, twenty twenty-two. The S and P five hundred, days off its all-time high, printed five straight lower closes. The rule fired. Filled the next day at four sixty-five ninety-seven. Five sessions later it hit the stop. Minus one R, exactly as designed. Then the market kept falling for nine more months, another twenty-five percent. The rule fired four times in twenty twenty-two and was stopped in three of them. The loss control worked every time: each failure cost about one R. What failed was the premise. In a downtrend, the sixth day down is just Tuesday.

context_2022.note: Jun 14 -1.0R, Dec 7 -1.0R; the one winner Jul 14 +3.5R; QQQ Dec 20 -1.0R and TSLA Aug 22 -1.52R also stopped that year

segments.editorial_rules: frame-vs-price only; synth voice only; publish every outcome; split-adjusted bars only
segments.why: Owner direction 2026-07-12: frame the failure, don't just say the caveat. The framed failure shows the losses were contained (-1R each) while the premise failed — that IS the lesson.
